rubric.requirements: The core contributions of 'SmoothQuant: Accurate and Efficient Post-Training Quantization for Large Language Models' have been reproduced, including the SmoothQuant per-channel smoothing transformation, the O1-O3 quantization settings, the W8A8 INT8 quantization flow for transformer blocks, and the accuracy, speedup, and memory experiments across OPT, BLOOM, GLM-130B, and other LLMs.

sub_tasks.requirements: The integer INT8 uniform quantization primitives and granularity levels (per-tensor, per-token, per-channel, group-wise; static vs dynamic) have been implemented as described in the paper's Preliminaries.

The INT8 uniform quantization formula from Equation 1 has been implemented: X_INT8 = round(X_FP16 / Delta), where Delta = max(|X|) / (2^(N-1) - 1) with N=8, using symmetric quantization around 0 with rounding to nearest integer.

Both static quantization (where the quantization step size Delta is computed offline from calibration samples) and dynamic quantization (where Delta is computed at runtime from the actual tensor statistics) have been implemented as two distinct modes, as described in Section 2.
Per-tensor quantization has been implemented, using a single scalar quantization step size Delta for the entire weight or activation matrix, as described in Section 2 and Figure 3.

Per-token quantization has been implemented for activations, using a separate quantization step size Delta for each token (i.e., each row of the activation matrix X of shape T x C_i), as described in Section 2 and Figure 3.

Per-channel quantization has been implemented for weights, using a separate quantization step size Delta for each output channel of the weight matrix W (i.e., each column of W of shape C_i x C_o), as described in Section 2 and Figure 3.

Group-wise quantization has been implemented as a coarser version of per-channel quantization, where different quantization step sizes are used for different channel groups within the weight matrix, as described in Section 2.

The hardware-compatible post-GEMM rescaling from Equation 2 has been implemented: Y = diag(Delta_X^FP16) * (X_INT8 * W_INT8) * diag(Delta_W^FP16), where scaling is applied only along the outer dimensions of the matrix multiplication (token dimension T for activations, output channel dimension C_o for weights) after the INT8 GEMM finishes.
The review of quantization difficulty has been reproduced, including the activation outlier observations and the simulated per-channel activation quantization comparison in Table 1.

The input activations and weights of a linear layer with large quantization error have been visualized as in Figure 4 (left), showing that activation distributions contain outliers ~100x larger than typical values while weight distributions are flat and uniform, and that outliers persist in fixed channels across all tokens (red channels in Figure 4).

The code or analysis demonstrates that weight distributions are uniform and flat (easy to quantize), while activation distributions contain large outliers that make quantization difficult, consistent with the three observations enumerated in Section 3: (1) activations are harder to quantize than weights, (2) outliers dominate per-tensor quantization step size causing low effective quantization levels (2-3 levels) for non-outlier channels, and (3) outliers persist in fixed channels across all tokens.

Simulated INT8 per-tensor, per-token, and per-channel activation quantization have been implemented and applied to OPT models (6.7B, 13B, 30B, 66B, 175B), with accuracy evaluated as average accuracy on WinoGrande, HellaSwag, PIQA, and LAMBADA, to reproduce Table 1.
Table 1 reports FP16 baseline average accuracy on WinoGrande, HellaSwag, PIQA, and LAMBADA for OPT models: 64.9% (6.7B), 65.6% (13B), 67.9% (30B), 69.5% (66B), 71.6% (175B).

Table 1 reports INT8 per-tensor quantization average accuracy for OPT models: 39.9% (6.7B), 33.0% (13B), 32.8% (30B), 33.1% (66B), 32.3% (175B), showing severe accuracy degradation across all model sizes.

Table 1 reports INT8 per-token quantization average accuracy for OPT models: 42.5% (6.7B), 33.0% (13B), 33.1% (30B), 32.9% (66B), 31.7% (175B), showing that per-token quantization provides only marginal improvement over per-tensor and fails to address activation quantization difficulty.

Table 1 reports INT8 per-channel quantization average accuracy for OPT models: 64.8% (6.7B), 65.6% (13B), 68.0% (30B), 69.4% (66B), 71.4% (175B), showing near-perfect accuracy recovery matching FP16 baseline, while also noting that per-channel quantization is marked as incompatible (gray) with INT8 GEMM kernels.

The code or analysis documents that per-channel activation quantization is incompatible with hardware-accelerated INT8 GEMM kernels (e.g., Tensor Core MMAs) because scaling along the inner channel dimension requires lower-throughput operations (e.g., CUDA Core FMAs) that cannot be inserted within the high-throughput kernel sequence, as described in Section 3.

The SmoothQuant per-channel smoothing transformation with migration strength alpha has been implemented, including the mathematically equivalent scaling, factor fusion into previous layers, and application to transformer blocks.

The mathematically equivalent per-channel smoothing transformation from Equation 3 has been implemented: Y = (X * diag(s)^{-1}) * (diag(s) * W) = X_hat * W_hat, where s ∈ R^{C_i} is a per-channel smoothing factor, X_hat = X * diag(s)^{-1} is the smoothed activation, and W_hat = diag(s) * W is the correspondingly scaled weight matrix, ensuring the linear layer output Y is identical before and after the transformation.

The per-channel smoothing factor formula from Equation 4 has been implemented: s_j = max(|X_j|)^alpha / max(|W_j|)^(1-alpha), where alpha ∈ [0,1] is the migration strength hyperparameter, j indexes the input channel (j=1,...,C_i), and max(|X_j|) is estimated from calibration samples. The default alpha=0.5 is used for OPT and BLOOM models, alpha=0.75 for GLM-130B, and alpha=0.8 for LLaMA models.

The per-channel activation scale max(|X_j|) is estimated offline using 512 random calibration sentences from the Pile pre-training dataset, as described in Section 5.1. The smoothing factors s and static quantization step sizes are computed once from these calibration samples and fixed for all downstream task evaluations.

The smoothing factor diag(s)^{-1} has been fused offline into the parameters of the preceding layer (e.g., linear layers, layer norms) so that no extra runtime kernel call overhead is incurred. For cases where the input comes from a residual addition, an extra scaling is applied to the residual branch, as described in Section 4.

The quantization flow for transformer blocks shown in Figure 6 has been implemented: the inputs and weights of all compute-heavy operators (linear layers in self-attention and feed-forward networks, and BMM operators in the attention computation) are quantized to INT8 (W8A8), while lightweight element-wise operations including ReLU, Softmax, and LayerNorm keep their activations in FP16.

SmoothQuant's per-channel scale smoothing is applied to the input activations of both the self-attention linear layers and the feed-forward (FFN) linear layers by default, covering all linear layers in the transformer block as described in Section 4.

The batched matrix multiplication (BMM) operators in the attention computation (i.e., Q*K^T and attention_weights*V) are quantized to INT8 using CUTLASS INT8 GEMM kernels, as described in Section 4 and Section 5.1.

The full SmoothQuant offline transformation pipeline has been implemented: (1) collect activation statistics max(|X_j|) from calibration samples, (2) compute per-channel smoothing factors s_j using Equation 4, (3) fuse diag(s)^{-1} into the preceding layer's parameters, (4) absorb diag(s) into the current layer's weight matrix W, and (5) produce a quantized model where both weights and activations are INT8 without any runtime overhead from the smoothing operation.
The O1-O3 SmoothQuant quantization settings and the four baseline quantization schemes (W8A8, ZeroQuant, LLM.int8(), Outlier Suppression) have been implemented per Table 2.

The SmoothQuant-O1 quantization setting has been implemented as described in Table 2: per-tensor static quantization for both weights and activations (W8A8, per-tensor, static), where a single scalar quantization step size Delta is used for the entire weight matrix and the entire activation matrix, computed offline from calibration samples.

The SmoothQuant-O2 quantization setting has been implemented as described in Table 2: per-tensor static quantization for weights and per-token dynamic quantization for activations (W8A8, per-tensor weight / per-token activation), where a separate quantization step size is computed per token at runtime for activations and a single static per-tensor step size is used for weights.

The SmoothQuant-O3 quantization setting has been implemented as described in Table 2: per-channel static quantization for weights and per-token dynamic quantization for activations (W8A8, per-channel weight / per-token activation), where a separate quantization step size is used per output channel for weights (computed offline) and per token for activations (computed at runtime).

The naive W8A8 baseline quantization scheme has been implemented as described in Table 2: per-tensor static quantization for both weights and activations without any smoothing transformation, serving as the lower bound for accuracy before applying SmoothQuant.

The ZeroQuant baseline quantization scheme has been implemented as described in Table 2: per-token dynamic quantization for activations and group-wise quantization for weights (following the ZeroQuant paper by Yao et al., 2022), without SmoothQuant's per-channel smoothing transformation.

The LLM.int8() baseline quantization scheme has been implemented as described in Table 2: it decomposes the matrix multiplication into a mixed-precision scheme where outlier channels are kept in FP16 and non-outlier channels are quantized to INT8, following Dettmers et al., 2022, without SmoothQuant's per-channel smoothing transformation.

The Outlier Suppression baseline quantization scheme has been implemented as described in Table 2, following Wei et al., 2022, which suppresses activation outliers through a different approach than SmoothQuant's channel-wise migration, as a W8A8 baseline comparison.

All quantization settings (naive W8A8, ZeroQuant, LLM.int8(), Outlier Suppression, SmoothQuant-O1, O2, O3) are organized and verifiable against Table 2's columns specifying weight quantization granularity, activation quantization granularity, static vs dynamic calibration mode, and whether the SmoothQuant smoothing transformation is applied.

The experimental setup has been reproduced, including model families, evaluation datasets, calibration procedure, migration strength selection, and the PyTorch/FasterTransformer implementations with CUTLASS INT8 kernels.

The three families of LLMs evaluated in the paper have been acquired and are runnable: OPT (Zhang et al., 2022) models at sizes 6.7B, 13B, 30B, 66B, and 175B; BLOOM (Scao et al., 2022) at 176B; and GLM-130B (Zeng et al., 2022) at 130B, as specified in Section 5.1.

The seven zero-shot evaluation benchmarks used for OPT and BLOOM models have been configured and are runnable via lm-eval-harness: LAMBADA (Paperno et al., 2016), HellaSwag (Zellers et al., 2019), PIQA (Bisk et al., 2020), WinoGrande (Sakaguchi et al., 2019), OpenBookQA (Mihaylov et al., 2018), RTE (Wang et al., 2018), and COPA (Roemmele et al., 2011), plus WikiText (Merity et al., 2016) as a language modeling dataset, as specified in Section 5.1.

The four evaluation benchmarks used for GLM-130B have been configured and are runnable via GLM-130B's official repository: MMLU (Hendrycks et al., 2020), MNLI (Williams et al., 2018), QNLI (Wang et al., 2018), and LAMBADA (Paperno et al., 2016), as specified in Section 5.1 (these differ from the OPT/BLOOM datasets because some benchmarks appear in GLM-130B's training set).

The calibration procedure has been implemented to collect 512 random sentences from the Pile (Gao et al., 2020) pre-training dataset validation set, compute per-channel activation statistics max(|X_j|) and static quantization step sizes once from these samples, and apply the same smoothed and quantized model for all downstream tasks without re-calibrating per task, as described in Section 5.1.

The migration strength alpha selection procedure has been implemented as a grid search on a subset of the Pile validation set, resulting in alpha=0.5 for all OPT and BLOOM models, alpha=0.75 for GLM-130B (which has ~30% outliers making activation quantization more difficult), and alpha=0.8 for LLaMA models, as described in Section 5.1 and Section 4.

The PyTorch Huggingface backend implementation of SmoothQuant has been implemented, including INT8 linear modules and batched matrix multiplication (BMM) functions using CUTLASS INT8 GEMM kernels, where the original FP16 linear modules and bmm function are replaced with INT8 kernels, as described in Section 5.1. This backend is used for proof-of-concept and single-GPU benchmarks on OPT-6.7B, OPT-13B, and OPT-30B.

The FasterTransformer backend implementation of SmoothQuant has been implemented, including INT8 linear modules and BMM functions with CUTLASS INT8 GEMM kernels integrated into the FasterTransformer serving framework, with support for Tensor Parallelism (Shoeybi et al., 2019) for multi-GPU serving of OPT-13B, OPT-30B, OPT-66B, and OPT-175B, as described in Section 5.1.

All benchmarking experiments are conducted on NVIDIA A100 80GB GPU servers, as specified in Section 5.3. Multi-GPU configurations include: 1 GPU for OPT up to 30B (single-GPU experiments), 2 GPUs for OPT-66B (FP16 baseline), 1 GPU for OPT-66B (SmoothQuant), 8 GPUs for OPT-175B (FP16 baseline), and 4 GPUs for OPT-175B (SmoothQuant).

The additional LLM families evaluated in Tables 6 and 7 have been acquired and are runnable: LLaMA models (Touvron et al., 2023a) at 7B, 13B, 30B, 65B; Llama-2 (Touvron et al., 2023b) at 7B, 13B, 70B; Falcon (Almazrouei et al., 2023) at 7B and 40B; Mistral (Jiang et al., 2023) at 7B; and Mixtral (Jiang et al., 2024) 8x7B, as specified in Section 5.2.

The WikiText-2 perplexity evaluation has been configured with a sequence length of 512 for LLaMA models (Table 6) and a sequence length of 2048 for Llama-2, Falcon, Mistral, and Mixtral models (Table 7), as specified in the captions of Tables 6 and 7 respectively.
The accuracy-preserving quantization results across OPT-175B, various LLMs, different OPT sizes, instruction-tuned, LLaMA, Llama-2, Falcon, Mistral, and Mixtral models have been reproduced.

SmoothQuant quantization of OPT-175B has been executed under all configurations (FP16, naive W8A8, ZeroQuant, LLM.int8(), Outlier Suppression, SmoothQuant-O1, SmoothQuant-O2, SmoothQuant-O3) and evaluated on all 7 zero-shot benchmarks (LAMBADA, HellaSwag, PIQA, WinoGrande, OpenBookQA, RTE, COPA) plus WikiText perplexity to reproduce Table 3.
Table 3 reports FP16 baseline results for OPT-175B: LAMBADA 74.7%, HellaSwag 59.3%, PIQA 79.7%, WinoGrande 72.6%, OpenBookQA 34.0%, RTE 59.9%, COPA 88.0%, Average 66.9%, WikiText perplexity 10.99.

Table 3 reports naive W8A8 results for OPT-175B: LAMBADA 0.0%, HellaSwag 25.6%, PIQA 53.4%, WinoGrande 50.3%, OpenBookQA 14.0%, RTE 49.5%, COPA 56.0%, Average 35.5%, WikiText perplexity 93080, showing catastrophic accuracy degradation.

Table 3 reports ZeroQuant results for OPT-175B: LAMBADA 0.0%, HellaSwag 26.0%, PIQA 51.7%, WinoGrande 49.3%, OpenBookQA 17.8%, RTE 50.9%, COPA 55.0%, Average 35.8%, WikiText perplexity 84648, including the note that even leaving input activations of self-attention in FP16 does not resolve accuracy degradation.

Table 3 reports LLM.int8() results for OPT-175B: LAMBADA 74.7%, HellaSwag 59.2%, PIQA 79.7%, WinoGrande 72.1%, OpenBookQA 34.2%, RTE 60.3%, COPA 87.0%, Average 66.7%, WikiText perplexity 11.10, matching FP16 accuracy.

Table 3 reports Outlier Suppression results for OPT-175B: LAMBADA 0.00%, HellaSwag 25.8%, PIQA 52.5%, WinoGrande 48.6%, OpenBookQA 16.6%, RTE 53.4%, COPA 55.0%, Average 36.0%, WikiText perplexity 96151, showing catastrophic accuracy degradation.
Table 3 reports SmoothQuant-O1 results for OPT-175B: LAMBADA 74.7%, HellaSwag 59.2%, PIQA 79.7%, WinoGrande 71.2%, OpenBookQA 33.4%, RTE 58.1%, COPA 89.0%, Average 66.5%, WikiText perplexity 11.11.
Table 3 reports SmoothQuant-O2 results for OPT-175B: LAMBADA 75.0%, HellaSwag 59.0%, PIQA 79.2%, WinoGrande 71.2%, OpenBookQA 33.0%, RTE 59.6%, COPA 88.0%, Average 66.4%, WikiText perplexity 11.14.

Table 3 reports SmoothQuant-O3 results for OPT-175B: LAMBADA 74.6%, HellaSwag 58.9%, PIQA 79.7%, WinoGrande 71.2%, OpenBookQA 33.4%, RTE 59.9%, COPA 90.0%, Average 66.8%, WikiText perplexity 11.17, demonstrating that even the most aggressive O3 setting maintains near-FP16 accuracy.

SmoothQuant quantization has been executed for OPT-175B, BLOOM-176B, and GLM-130B under all configurations (FP16, naive W8A8, ZeroQuant, LLM.int8(), Outlier Suppression, SmoothQuant-O1, O2, O3) and evaluated on the appropriate benchmark sets for each model to reproduce Table 4.

Table 4 reports OPT-175B average accuracy (WinoGrande, HellaSwag, PIQA, LAMBADA) across methods: FP16 71.6%, W8A8 32.3%, ZeroQuant 31.7%, LLM.int8() 71.4%, Outlier Suppression 31.7%, SmoothQuant-O1 71.2%, SmoothQuant-O2 71.1%, SmoothQuant-O3 71.1%.

Table 4 reports BLOOM-176B average accuracy (WinoGrande, HellaSwag, PIQA, LAMBADA) across methods: FP16 68.2%, W8A8 64.2%, ZeroQuant 67.4%, LLM.int8() 68.0%, Outlier Suppression 54.1%, SmoothQuant-O1 68.3%, SmoothQuant-O2 68.4%, SmoothQuant-O3 67.4%, consistent with BLOOM being easier to quantize than OPT-175B.

Table 4 reports GLM-130B average accuracy (LAMBADA, MMLU, MNLI, QNLI) across methods: FP16 73.8%, W8A8 26.9%, ZeroQuant 26.7%, LLM.int8() 73.8%, Outlier Suppression 63.5%, SmoothQuant-O1 73.7%, SmoothQuant-O2 72.5%, SmoothQuant-O3 72.8%, with the note that top 2% tokens are clipped during static quantization calibration for GLM-130B.

SmoothQuant has been applied and evaluated on all OPT model sizes (covering the scale range from small to 175B) to produce the results shown in Figure 7, demonstrating that SmoothQuant consistently matches FP16 accuracy with INT8 quantization across all OPT model scales.

Figure 7 has been reproduced as a plot showing average accuracy (or perplexity) vs. OPT model size for FP16 and SmoothQuant INT8, demonstrating that SmoothQuant matches FP16 accuracy consistently across all OPT model sizes from small to 175B.

SmoothQuant quantization of OPT-IML-30B has been executed under FP16, W8A8, ZeroQuant, LLM.int8(), Outlier Suppression, and SmoothQuant-O3 configurations and evaluated on LAMBADA and WikiText-2 to reproduce Table 5.

Table 5 reports OPT-IML-30B results: FP16 LAMBADA 69.12% / WikiText 14.26; W8A8 LAMBADA 4.21% / WikiText 576.53; ZeroQuant LAMBADA 5.12% / WikiText 455.12; LLM.int8() LAMBADA 69.14% / WikiText 14.27; Outlier Suppression LAMBADA 0.00% / WikiText 9485.62; SmoothQuant-O3 LAMBADA 69.77% / WikiText 14.37.

SmoothQuant W8A8 quantization of LLaMA models (7B, 13B, 30B, 65B) has been executed using per-token activation quantization and alpha=0.8, evaluated on WikiText-2 perplexity with sequence length 512, to reproduce Table 6.

Table 6 reports WikiText-2 perplexity for LLaMA models: FP16 7B=11.51, 13B=10.05, 30B=7.53, 65B=6.17; W8A8 SmoothQuant 7B=11.56, 13B=10.08, 30B=7.56, 65B=6.20, demonstrating lossless W8A8 quantization with negligible perplexity increase.

SmoothQuant W8A8 quantization has been executed for Llama-2 (7B, 13B, 70B), Falcon (7B, 40B), Mistral-7B, and Mixtral-8x7B using per-token activation quantization and per-channel weight quantization, evaluated on WikiText-2 perplexity with sequence length 2048, with model-specific alpha values, to reproduce Table 7.

Table 7 reports WikiText-2 perplexity for Llama-2 models: Llama-2-7B FP16=5.474, W8A8 SQ=5.515 (alpha=0.85); Llama-2-13B FP16=4.950, W8A8 SQ=4.929 (alpha=0.85); Llama-2-70B FP16=3.320, W8A8 SQ=3.359 (alpha=0.9).
Table 7 reports WikiText-2 perplexity for Falcon models: Falcon-7B FP16=6.590, W8A8 SQ=6.629 (alpha=0.6); Falcon-40B FP16=5.228, W8A8 SQ=5.255 (alpha=0.7).

Table 7 reports WikiText-2 perplexity for Mistral-7B: FP16=5.253, W8A8 SQ=5.277 (alpha=0.8); and Mixtral-8x7B (MoE): FP16=3.842, W8A8 SQ=3.893 (alpha=0.8), demonstrating SmoothQuant works on diverse architectures including Mixture of Experts models.
The speedup and memory saving results for SmoothQuant-O3 in the PyTorch and FasterTransformer backends across context and decoding stages have been reproduced.

The PyTorch backend latency benchmarks have been executed for FP16 and SmoothQuant-O3 INT8 on OPT models (6.7B, 13B, 30B) for both the context (prefill) stage and the decoding (generation) stage on a single NVIDIA A100 80GB GPU, measuring per-batch latency to reproduce Figure 8.

Figure 8 reports PyTorch backend context-stage speedup of SmoothQuant-O3 over FP16 for OPT models: OPT-6.7B achieves approximately 1.51x speedup, OPT-13B achieves approximately 1.56x speedup, and OPT-30B achieves approximately 1.51x speedup in the context (prefill) stage, consistent with Figure 8 of the paper.

Figure 8 reports PyTorch backend decoding-stage speedup of SmoothQuant-O3 over FP16 for OPT models: OPT-6.7B achieves approximately 1.03x speedup, OPT-13B achieves approximately 1.03x speedup, and OPT-30B achieves approximately 1.03x speedup in the decoding (generation) stage, with the note that decoding is memory-bandwidth-bound and thus benefits less from INT8 computation than the compute-bound context stage, consistent with Figure 8.

The FasterTransformer backend latency benchmarks have been executed for FP16 and SmoothQuant-O3 INT8 on OPT models (13B, 30B, 66B, 175B) across both context and decoding stages using multi-GPU configurations on NVIDIA A100 80GB GPUs, to reproduce Figure 9.

Figure 9 reports FasterTransformer backend context-stage speedup of SmoothQuant-O3 over FP16 for OPT models: OPT-13B achieves approximately 1.56x speedup, OPT-30B achieves approximately 1.55x speedup, OPT-66B achieves approximately 1.62x speedup, and OPT-175B achieves approximately 1.51x speedup in the context (prefill) stage, consistent with Figure 9 of the paper.

Figure 9 reports FasterTransformer backend decoding-stage speedup of SmoothQuant-O3 over FP16 for OPT models: OPT-13B, OPT-30B, OPT-66B, and OPT-175B each achieve approximately 1.0x–1.12x speedup in the decoding (generation) stage, consistent with Figure 9 of the paper, reflecting that decoding is memory-bandwidth-bound.

The GPU memory consumption measurements have been executed for FP16 and SmoothQuant-O3 INT8 on OPT models (13B, 30B, 66B, 175B) using both PyTorch and FasterTransformer backends, measuring peak GPU memory in GB and the number of A100 80GB GPUs required per model configuration, to reproduce Table 8.

Table 8 reports GPU memory usage for FP16 vs SmoothQuant-O3 INT8: OPT-13B FP16 requires 28.0 GB (1 GPU) vs INT8 14.0 GB (1 GPU); OPT-30B FP16 requires 63.5 GB (2 GPUs) vs INT8 31.8 GB (1 GPU); OPT-66B FP16 requires 141.5 GB (2 GPUs) vs INT8 70.7 GB (1 GPU); OPT-175B FP16 requires 346.1 GB (8 GPUs, FasterTransformer) vs INT8 173.1 GB (4 GPUs), demonstrating approximately 2x memory reduction and halving of required GPU count for large models, consistent with Table 8.

The speedup bar charts corresponding to Figures 8 and 9 have been generated, showing per-model speedup ratios (SmoothQuant-O3 vs FP16) separately for the context stage and decoding stage in both the PyTorch and FasterTransformer backends, with model sizes on the x-axis and speedup ratio on the y-axis.

The analysis confirms that SmoothQuant-O3 INT8 quantization reduces the number of A100 80GB GPUs required to serve OPT-30B from 2 to 1, OPT-66B from 2 to 1, and OPT-175B from 8 to 4, as reported in Table 8 and discussed in Section 5.3, demonstrating that SmoothQuant enables serving larger models on fewer GPUs.
The scaling-up experiment quantizing MT-NLG 530B to W8A8 with negligible accuracy loss and half-GPU serving has been reproduced.

The MT-NLG 530B model (Smith et al., 2022) has been acquired and is runnable on the target hardware configuration (16× A100 80GB GPUs for FP16, 8× A100 80GB GPUs for SmoothQuant INT8), as described in Section 5.4.

SmoothQuant W8A8 quantization has been applied to MT-NLG 530B and evaluated under both FP16 (baseline) and INT8 (SmoothQuant) configurations on the four zero-shot benchmarks: LAMBADA, HellaSwag, PIQA, and WinoGrande, to reproduce Table 9.
Table 9 reports FP16 baseline accuracy for MT-NLG 530B: LAMBADA 76.6%, HellaSwag 62.1%, PIQA 81.0%, WinoGrande 72.9%, Average 73.1%.
Table 9 reports SmoothQuant INT8 (W8A8) accuracy for MT-NLG 530B: LAMBADA 77.2%, HellaSwag 60.4%, PIQA 80.7%, WinoGrande 74.1%, Average 73.1%, demonstrating negligible accuracy loss relative to FP16.

Latency and GPU memory measurements have been collected for MT-NLG 530B in the decoding stage under FP16 (16× A100 80GB GPUs) and SmoothQuant INT8 (8× A100 80GB GPUs) configurations across batch sizes (BS=1, BS=16) and sequence lengths (512, 1024), to reproduce Table 10 (referred to as Table 8 in the relevant paper section but corresponding to the decoding-stage performance table for 530B).

Table 10 (the decoding-stage table for 530B scaling) reports MT-NLG 530B decoding-stage results demonstrating that SmoothQuant reduces the required GPU count from 16 to 8 A100 80GB GPUs at similar latency, enabling serving of the >500B model within a single node (8×A100 80GB GPUs), consistent with Section 5.4.

The analysis confirms that SmoothQuant W8A8 quantization of MT-NLG 530B reduces the number of A100 80GB GPUs required for serving from 16 (FP16) to 8 (INT8), enabling the entire 530B model to be served within a single node of 8×A100 80GB GPUs at similar latency, as stated in Section 5.4.
The ablation studies on quantization schemes latency and migration strength alpha have been reproduced.

The inference latency benchmarks for all quantization schemes (LLM.int8(), FP16 baseline, SmoothQuant-O1, SmoothQuant-O2, SmoothQuant-O3) have been executed using the PyTorch implementation on OPT models, measuring per-batch latency to reproduce Table 11.

Table 11 reports that LLM.int8() is usually slower than the FP16 baseline across all quantization granularity settings (O1, O2, O3), consistent with Section 5.5's statement that 'LLM.int8() is usually slower'.

Table 11 reports that SmoothQuant is faster than the FP16 baseline under all quantization granularity settings (O1, O2, O3), consistent with Section 5.5's statement that 'SmoothQuant is faster than FP16 baseline under all settings'.

Table 11 reports that coarser quantization granularity yields lower inference latency, i.e., SmoothQuant-O1 (per-tensor static) has lower latency than SmoothQuant-O2 (per-tensor weight / per-token activation dynamic), which in turn has lower latency than SmoothQuant-O3 (per-channel weight / per-token activation dynamic), consistent with Section 5.5's statement that 'the coarser the quantization granularity (from O1 to O3), the lower the latency'.

Table 11 reports that static quantization achieves significantly lower latency than dynamic quantization, consistent with Section 5.5's statement that 'static quantization can significantly accelerate inference compared with dynamic quantization because we no longer need to calculate the quantization step sizes at runtime'.
A sweep of migration strength alpha values has been executed on OPT-175B, evaluating LAMBADA accuracy for each alpha value to reproduce Figure 10.

Figure 10 has been generated as a plot showing LAMBADA accuracy vs. migration strength alpha for OPT-175B, with the x-axis showing alpha values and the y-axis showing model performance, clearly indicating the sweet spot region (0.4–0.6).

Figure 10 shows that when alpha < 0.4, LAMBADA accuracy degrades significantly because the activations remain hard to quantize (insufficient migration of quantization difficulty to weights), consistent with Section 5.5.

Figure 10 shows that when alpha > 0.6, LAMBADA accuracy degrades significantly because the weights become hard to quantize (too much quantization difficulty migrated to weights), consistent with Section 5.5.

Figure 10 shows that alpha values in the range [0.4, 0.6] form a sweet spot where both activations and weights are quantizable with small quantization errors, maintaining model performance after quantization on OPT-175B LAMBADA, consistent with Section 5.5.
